era_context: Late 14th century: Ottoman Empire consolidating Balkan territories; Ming Dynasty under Zhu Yuanzhang's final years (died 1398); Goryeo Dynasty in terminal decline in Korea; Western Schism ongoing in Catholic Church; Timur's empire at its height in Central Asia; English Peasants' Revolt aftermath (1381) still shaping social tensions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands and Oceania (no contemporary records)
Southeast Asia (limited European documentation; local records sparse in surviving sources)

events.title: Yi Seong-gye's military coup and rise to power
events.description: Yi Seong-gye, a military general, led a coup against the Goryeo court, removing the pro-Mongol faction and establishing himself as the dominant political force. This coup effectively ended Goryeo's independent rule and set the stage for the formal founding of the Joseon Dynasty in 1392. Yi's actions were motivated by opposition to the Goryeo court's alliance with the Yuan/Ming and by his own ambitions to reform the state along Neo-Confucian lines.

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Kings)

events.certainty_note: Yi Seong-gye's coup is well-documented in Korean historical records. The date and sequence of events are clearly established in the Joseon Wangjo Sillok.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans continues
events.description: Under Murad I's continued leadership, Ottoman forces expanded their control over Serbian and Bulgarian territories. The year 1388 represents the penultimate year of Murad I's reign before his death at Kosovo Polje in 1389. Ottoman military campaigns were systematically reducing Byzantine and Balkan Christian states' territories, establishing Ottoman dominance in the region.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion during Murad I's reign is well-documented, but specific events in 1388 are not individually detailed in surviving sources. This entry reflects the documented pattern of Ottoman military activity during this period.
cross_references: 1389-002 (Death of Murad I and succession of Bayezid I)

events.title: Ming Dynasty under Zhu Yuanzhang's final years
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang, the Hongwu Emperor and founder of the Ming Dynasty, was in the final decade of his reign (died 1398). In 1388, he was consolidating centralized power and eliminating potential rivals through purges. His reign saw the establishment of the Ming administrative system, the construction of the Forbidden City (begun 1406), and the development of Neo-Confucian state ideology. The year 1388 marks the mature phase of his rule.
events.certainty_note: Zhu Yuanzhang's reign and administrative activities are well-documented in Ming official records. The year 1388 falls within his documented period of centralization and purges.

events.title: Timur's consolidation of Central Asian empire
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) was consolidating his vast empire stretching from Central Asia to Anatolia. In 1388, he was establishing Samarkand as his capital and cultural center, commissioning architectural and artistic projects. His reign represented a synthesis of Mongol military traditions and Persian-Islamic cultural patronage. The year 1388 marks the height of his power before his later campaigns into the Levant and Egypt.
key_figures: Ulugh Beg (Timur's grandson, future astronomer and ruler)

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafar-nama (Book of Victories)

events.certainty_note: Timur's general activities and cultural patronage are well-documented, but specific events in 1388 are not precisely dated. Archaeological evidence from Samarkand confirms his building projects during this period.

events.title: Western Schism deepens in Catholic Church
events.description: The Western Schism (1378–1417) was ongoing, with rival papal claimants in Rome and Avignon. In 1388, Pope Urban VI (Roman claimant) was consolidating his authority, while the Avignon antipope Clement VII had died in 1394 (note: this requires verification for 1388 specifically). The schism was creating significant ecclesiastical and political divisions across Western Christendom, with different kingdoms recognizing different popes.

sources.name: Papal records and chronicles (Liber Pontificalis tradition)

events.certainty_note: The Western Schism and the reigns of Urban VI and Clement VII are well-documented in papal and ecclesiastical records. The year 1388 falls clearly within the schism period.
cross_references: 1417-001 (Council of Constance, end of Western Schism)

disconfirming_evidence: Yi Seong-gye's coup in 1388 is traditionally portrayed as a nationalist uprising against Mongol influence, but modern scholarship suggests the situation was more complex. Yi was not simply opposing 'foreign' rule but rather competing with other Korean factions for power. The Goryeo court's relationship with Yuan/Ming was not uniformly opposed by Korean elites. Additionally, the characterization of Yi's actions as a 'coup' may overstate the violence and disruption; the transition appears to have been more gradual than traditional narratives suggest. The Western Schism's religious significance has been emphasized in Western historiography, but its impact on non-Western Christian communities (Eastern Orthodox, Coptic, etc.) was minimal, suggesting a Western-centric bias in its historical prominence.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1388 is moderate but regionally uneven. East Asian sources (Ming Shilu, Joseon Wangjo Sillok) are contemporary and detailed. Ottoman and Central Asian sources are primarily later compilations. European ecclesiastical sources are contemporary but focus narrowly on papal politics. There is a significant Western bias in available documentation, with Ottoman, Central Asian, and East Asian sources receiving less attention in Western historiography. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania have no documented written records for this year. The year 1388 is less well-documented than 1389 (Battle of Kosovo) or 1392 (Joseon founding), suggesting that historical attention clusters around dramatic events rather than providing even coverage.

graph_edges.note: Yi Seong-gye's 1388 coup directly led to the formal founding of Joseon in 1392
Ottoman expansion in 1388 set conditions for the Battle of Kosovo Polje in 1389
Zhu Yuanzhang's consolidation in 1388 established the Ming system that would continue after his death in 1398
The Western Schism deepening in 1388 contributed to the crisis resolved at the Council of Constance in 1417
